content.text: Reproducibility in language-model outputs depends on two cooperating mechanisms: fixing the pseudo-random number generator with an integer seed and minimising sampling variance with a low temperature. Together they let engineers replay a specific generation, stabilise evaluation pipelines, and debug errant agent behaviour — while accepting that hardware and model-version changes remain outside any single parameter's control.
What it is

A seed is an integer passed to the inference API alongside the usual generation parameters. It initialises the pseudo-random number generator (PRNG) used during token sampling, so that — given identical model weights, the same prompt, and the same infrastructure — the model produces the same sequence of tokens on every call. The concept originates in numerical computing: fixing the RNG state before a stochastic process makes that process deterministic, at least within the boundaries of a single software run.

Most LLM providers implement seed on a best-effort basis. OpenAI, for example, exposes a seed field and returns a system_fingerprint in the response that encodes the model version and backend configuration active at the time of the call. When the fingerprint stays constant across two calls, the same seed should produce identical output. When it changes — because the model was silently updated or the serving cluster changed — determinism is not guaranteed even with the same seed. Anthropic's Claude API does not expose a seed parameter at all, relying instead on temperature=0 for near-determinism.
Why it matters

Non-determinism is the default in LLM inference: sampling with temperature > 0 means that the same prompt can yield meaningfully different outputs across calls. For user-facing applications that is often desirable — diversity, creativity, and non-repetition depend on it. But for engineering workflows the randomness is a liability.

Consider a CI pipeline that validates whether a refactored system prompt still produces well-structured JSON. Without a fixed seed the test might pass 95 % of the time and fail the remaining 5 %, making it a flaky test rather than a reliable gate. With a seed and temperature=0 the test becomes repeatable: a failure always indicates a real regression, not random sampling noise. Eval harnesses, golden-output snapshots, prompt-regression suites, and offline debugging all depend on this kind of repeatability.

Determinism also matters when comparing two model versions or prompt variants. Holding the seed constant across an A/B comparison removes sampling variance from the measurement, so any difference in output quality can be attributed cleanly to the change being tested rather than to random draw variation.
Benefits
Using seeds correctly gives engineering teams several concrete advantages:
Stable CI/CD gates.
A seeded test either passes or fails consistently, eliminating the category of flaky tests caused purely by sampling variance.
Accurate prompt A/B comparisons.
When the seed is held constant, two prompt variants are evaluated on exactly the same draws from the model's distribution, making quality differences statistically attributable to the prompt change.
Reproducible debugging.
If an agent emits a bad action in production, replaying the same request with the same seed on the same model version reproduces the exact failure, so a developer can inspect it step by step.
Snapshot testing.
Teams can store expected outputs as golden files and assert that a code change does not alter them — a pattern analogous to snapshot tests in front-end frameworks.
Eval harness cost control.

Running a large evaluation suite is expensive. A deterministic run can be cached: if the prompt, seed, model, and fingerprint all match a previous run, the result can be re-used without another API call.
Controlled ablation studies.
Researchers can change one variable at a time in a prompt and hold all randomness constant across conditions.
Trade-offs
Seed-based determinism comes with important caveats that every practitioner needs to understand before relying on it:
Best-effort, not guaranteed.
Providers explicitly document seed support as best-effort. GPU floating-point operations are not strictly associative, and parallel execution order can vary across calls.
Broken by model updates.

Providers silently update model weights (bug fixes, safety patches, alignment tuning). A call that returns the same system_fingerprint is reproducible; a new fingerprint means all previous seeds are invalidated.
Not a substitute for low temperature.
A seed with temperature=1.0 still samples stochastically — it merely fixes the sequence of random draws. True repeatability requires temperature=0 (greedy) or a very small temperature (≤ 0.1).
Platform-locked.

A seed produces the same output on one provider's infrastructure; switching from OpenAI to Azure OpenAI or to a local model with the same weights will not reproduce the same output because the RNG implementation differs.
Inhibits diversity.
Seeds are inappropriate for any use case where output variety is desirable, such as creative writing, chat assistants, or multi-sample generation pipelines that aggregate diverse responses.
Exact-string fragility.
Even within a single provider, trivial infrastructure changes can shift outputs by one token. Tests asserting exact strings will break; tests asserting structural properties will not.
How it works

At its core, token sampling is a stochastic process. After the transformer computes a logit vector over the vocabulary for each position, those logits are converted to a probability distribution and a token is drawn from that distribution. The draw uses a pseudo-random number generator. Without a seed, the PRNG is seeded from an unpredictable source (system entropy, timestamp) on every request. With a seed, the PRNG is initialised from the provided integer before sampling begins, so the sequence of draws is reproducible.
Greedy decoding versus seeded sampling

When temperature is set to 0, the model applies greedy decoding: at each step it selects the single highest-probability token (argmax). No random draw is involved, so a seed has no effect — the output is already as deterministic as the model's forward pass and the hardware allow. The residual non-determinism comes from floating-point non-associativity in GPU matrix operations, which can produce slightly different logit values when the computation graph is evaluated in different orders.
The softmax of logits
at temperature
is:
At T=0 this collapses to argmax selection. For T>0 the distribution retains spread, and sampling from it requires a random draw — precisely where the seed is consumed.
The role of system_fingerprint

OpenAI's API returns a system_fingerprint field alongside every completion. This string encodes the model version and backend configuration (kernel optimisations, quantisation level, etc.) that produced the output. Two responses with the same system_fingerprint were produced by identical infrastructure, so the same seed will reproduce the same output. A changed fingerprint is a signal that the environment drifted, and reproducibility is no longer guaranteed. Production eval tooling should persist this fingerprint alongside every stored result.
Architecture

The diagram below traces how a seed flows through an LLM inference pipeline and shows the conditions under which output determinism holds versus breaks. The dashed feedback arrow illustrates that a repeated call with the same parameters traverses the same path and produces the same token stream, provided the model fingerprint has not changed.

attrs.alt: Seed and determinism architecture diagram: request parameters → LLM engine (RNG state, logit computation, token sampling) → output tokens, with a feedback loop and panels showing when determinism holds versus breaks.

content.text: When to use it
Good candidates
Automated eval suites and benchmarks that compare prompt variants head to head.
CI/CD pipeline tests that assert a prompt template still produces valid structured output after a code change.
Golden-file snapshot tests: store expected output once, assert it does not regress.
Debugging a specific bad completion in production by replaying the exact request.
Offline batch scoring where identical requests should not be re-executed — cache by (seed, model, fingerprint, prompt).
Research experiments where prompt changes must be isolated from sampling variance.
When not to use it
User-facing conversational agents where diversity and non-repetition matter.
Creative writing or ideation pipelines that need varied outputs.
Multi-sample generation strategies (best-of-N, self-consistency) that depend on independent draws.
Any context where the provider does not document seed support — the parameter is silently ignored.
Situations where the exact bytes of the output matter across provider updates; use schema validation instead.
How to implement

Implementing deterministic generation requires coordinating three elements: the seed integer, a temperature of 0 (or close to it), and a pinned model version string. Omitting any one of these reduces or eliminates the determinism guarantee.

The recommended pattern is to centralise seed selection in a configuration object and pass it consistently to every call in an eval or test context, while leaving it absent for production user-facing calls.

import os
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI(api_key=os.environ["OPENAI_API_KEY"])

# Pin model version explicitly — never use a floating alias like "gpt-4o"
# for deterministic workflows, because it resolves to different weights
# after a provider update.
PINNED_MODEL = "gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18"
EVAL_SEED = 7777  # fixed across the entire eval run

def generate_deterministic(prompt: str, system: str = "") -> dict:
    """
    Returns the completion text and the system_fingerprint so callers
    can detect when provider infrastructure changes break determinism.
    """
    messages = []
    if system:
        messages.append({"role": "system", "content": system})
    messages.append({"role": "user", "content": prompt})

    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model=PINNED_MODEL,
        messages=messages,
        temperature=0,       # greedy — removes stochastic sampling entirely
        seed=EVAL_SEED,      # initialises PRNG for any residual sampling
        max_tokens=512,
    )
    return {
        "text": response.choices[0].message.content,
        "fingerprint": response.system_fingerprint,
        "model": response.model,
    }

result = generate_deterministic("Summarise the CAP theorem in two sentences.")
print(result["text"])
print("Fingerprint:", result["fingerprint"])

Always log or store the system_fingerprint alongside the generated output. If the fingerprint changes between two runs that should be comparable, flag the results as potentially non-comparable rather than silently merging them into the same evaluation bucket.
Examples
Example 1: Verifying reproducibility in Python

This minimal example demonstrates that the same seed, temperature, and model version produce identical output across two consecutive calls. The assertion will pass on a provider that honours the seed contract and fail if something in the infrastructure has shifted.

from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()

def call(seed: int) -> str:
    resp = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Name one key principle of SOLID design."}],
        temperature=0,
        seed=seed,
        max_tokens=60,
    )
    return resp.choices[0].message.content or ""

a = call(seed=1234)
b = call(seed=1234)
c = call(seed=9999)  # different seed

print("Run A:", a)
print("Run B:", b)
print("Identical (A==B):", a == b)    # Expected: True on stable infrastructure
print("Different (A==C):", a == c)    # Expected: False
Example 2: Seeded eval harness with fingerprint tracking

A realistic evaluation harness fixes the seed for each test case, stores the fingerprint, and warns when infrastructure drift is detected. It also uses JSON-mode output rather than exact-string matching, making the test robust to minor whitespace and punctuation variation.

import json, hashlib
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Any
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()

EVAL_MODEL = "gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18"
BASE_SEED  = 42

@dataclass
class EvalResult:
    prompt_id: str
    seed: int
    fingerprint: str
    output: Any          # parsed JSON
    passed: bool
    failure_reason: str = ""

def run_eval_case(prompt_id: str, prompt: str, validator) -> EvalResult:
    seed = BASE_SEED ^ (int(hashlib.md5(prompt_id.encode()).hexdigest(), 16) & 0xFFFF)
    resp = client.chat.completions.create(
        model=EVAL_MODEL,
        messages=[
            {"role": "system", "content": "Reply with valid JSON only. No prose."},
            {"role": "user",   "content": prompt},
        ],
        temperature=0,
        seed=seed,
        max_tokens=256,
        response_format={"type": "json_object"},
    )
    text = resp.choices[0].message.content or "{}"
    fp   = resp.system_fingerprint or "unknown"
    try:
        parsed = json.loads(text)
        ok, reason = validator(parsed)
    except json.JSONDecodeError as exc:
        return EvalResult(prompt_id, seed, fp, text, False, str(exc))
    return EvalResult(prompt_id, seed, fp, parsed, ok, reason)

# ── validator ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
def has_action_field(obj: dict):
    if "action" in obj and isinstance(obj["action"], str):
        return True, ""
    return False, f"Missing 'action' string key, got: {list(obj.keys())}"

result = run_eval_case(
    prompt_id="agent-action-001",
    prompt='Decide what to do: user asks "book a flight to Paris". '
           'Output {action, reason}.',
    validator=has_action_field,
)
print(f"[{result.prompt_id}] passed={result.passed} seed={result.seed}")
print(f"  fingerprint : {result.fingerprint}")
print(f"  output      : {result.output}")
if not result.passed:
    print(f"  failure     : {result.failure_reason}")
Real-world example

A team running weekly prompt-regression tests for a customer-support agent manages a suite of 500 golden test cases. Each case captures a user message, an expected structured response schema, and the seed and system_fingerprint that were active when the golden output was recorded. On each CI run the harness replays every case with the same seed against the current production model and compares schema conformance — not exact text — against the stored expectation.

When a system_fingerprint changes (indicating a model update), the harness automatically re-records all 500 golden outputs and creates a diff report that the team reviews before merging. This workflow catches regressions introduced by provider updates, prevents silent quality degradation, and takes less than four minutes to run because successful cases are cached by (model, fingerprint, seed, prompt_hash). The caching alone reduces eval cost by roughly 80 % on weeks when the model has not changed.

import hashlib, json, pathlib, time
from openai import OpenAI

client  = OpenAI()
CACHE   = pathlib.Path(".eval_cache")
CACHE.mkdir(exist_ok=True)

def cache_key(model: str, fp: str, seed: int, prompt: str) -> str:
    h = hashlib.sha256(f"{model}|{fp}|{seed}|{prompt}".encode()).hexdigest()[:16]
    return h

def cached_generate(prompt: str, seed: int, model: str) -> dict:
    # First call to get current fingerprint cheaply
    probe = client.chat.completions.create(
        model=model, messages=[{"role":"user","content":"ping"}],
        temperature=0, seed=seed, max_tokens=1,
    )
    fp  = probe.system_fingerprint or "unknown"
    key = cache_key(model, fp, seed, prompt)
    hit = CACHE / f"{key}.json"
    if hit.exists():
        return json.loads(hit.read_text())

    resp = client.chat.completions.create(
        model=model,
        messages=[{"role":"user","content":prompt}],
        temperature=0, seed=seed, max_tokens=256,
    )
    result = {
        "text": resp.choices[0].message.content,
        "fingerprint": fp,
        "model": resp.model,
        "ts": time.time(),
    }
    hit.write_text(json.dumps(result))
    return result

out = cached_generate("List 3 agent design patterns.", seed=42, model="gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18")
print(out["text"])
Common mistakes
Mistake 1: Using seed with temperature > 0 and expecting full determinism

A seed fixes the PRNG state but does not eliminate stochastic sampling. With temperature=0.7 the model still samples from a distribution — the seed only fixes which draws come out of the PRNG, which is useful for reducing variance but does not guarantee identical output across provider updates or hardware changes.

# BAD: seed with high temperature → stochastic, not deterministic
resp = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Write a haiku."}],
    temperature=0.9,   # <-- still sampling; output will vary across infra changes
    seed=42,
)

# GOOD: seed + temperature=0 for deterministic regression tests
resp = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Write a haiku."}],
    temperature=0,   # greedy; seed becomes the tiebreaker for any residual randomness
    seed=42,
)
Mistake 2: Using a floating model alias
Passing
model="gpt-4o-mini"

resolves to whatever weights the provider considers current. After a silent update, the alias points to new weights and all existing seeds produce different outputs. Always pin the full dated version string in eval contexts:

# BAD — alias resolves to different weights after provider update
model = "gpt-4o-mini"

# GOOD — explicit version locked to known weights
model = "gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18"
Mistake 3: Asserting exact output strings in tests

Even on a stable system_fingerprint, minor backend changes can shift token probabilities enough to change punctuation, capitalisation, or sentence order. Tests that assert exact bytes fail spuriously and require constant maintenance. The correct pattern is to validate the structure and semantics of the output, not the exact characters.

import json

# BAD — brittle exact-string assertion
assert output == "The key principle is single responsibility.", \
    "Output changed!"

# GOOD — structural assertion that tolerates minor wording variation
parsed = json.loads(output)
assert "principle" in parsed, "Response must include 'principle' key"
assert isinstance(parsed["principle"], str) and len(parsed["principle"]) > 5
Best practices
Applying the following practices keeps deterministic workflows reliable as models and infrastructure evolve:
Always pin the model version string.
Use dated aliases (e.g. gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18) in every eval and test config. Store the resolved model field from the API response, not the alias you sent.
Log system_fingerprint per response.
Record it in your eval database. Alert when it changes between scheduled runs, and automatically re-collect golden outputs when it does.
Combine seed with temperature=0.
Use both together for strongest determinism. Seed alone with high temperature does not guarantee reproducibility.
Assert structure, not exact text.
Validate JSON schema conformance, key presence, length ranges, and entity extraction accuracy. Avoid byte-identical string comparison.
Derive per-case seeds systematically.
Rather than using one global seed, derive a unique seed per test case (e.g. base_seed XOR hash(case_id)) so cases are independent and failures are isolated.
Cache by (seed, model, fingerprint, prompt_hash).
Do not re-call the API when all four match a stored result. This cuts eval cost significantly on stable infrastructure.
Document seed usage in configuration.
Make it clear in code comments and README entries that a seed is for eval/testing, not for production user-facing calls.
Test that your tests fail without the seed.
Periodically verify that removing the seed causes some tests to become non-deterministic. If they all still pass, the assertions may be too loose.
Performance & scalability

Seeded generation does not materially affect per-request latency or throughput — the PRNG initialisation cost is negligible compared with the forward pass. The performance concerns are at the system level.

Parallel batching introduces non-determinism that seed cannot fix. When a provider groups multiple requests into a single GPU kernel for efficiency, the order of floating-point accumulations changes. Even with the same seed and temperature=0, two calls that happen to be batched differently can produce different logit values due to floating-point non-associativity. This is why providers explicitly warn that seed support is best-effort rather than guaranteed.

For large eval suites the dominant cost is not per-call latency but total API expenditure. Response caching keyed on (model, fingerprint, seed, prompt_hash) addresses this: in practice, evaluation runs on unchanged prompts against an unchanged model can achieve 70–90 % cache hit rates, reducing cost proportionally. The cache entry should include the system_fingerprint so that a stale cache hit against a new model version is never served.

At very large scale — thousands of eval cases run in parallel — batching the API calls increases throughput but risks introducing the non-determinism described above. If strict reproducibility matters more than throughput, run eval calls sequentially or in small serial batches and record the fingerprint from each response.
Tooling & ecosystem
OpenAI Python SDK
The official SDK exposes
seed
as a keyword argument on
chat.completions.create
and returns
system_fingerprint
in the response object. Both are available in all gpt-4o and gpt-3.5-turbo variants that support seed.

from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()

resp = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "What is backpropagation?"}],
    temperature=0,
    seed=1001,
    max_tokens=128,
)
print(resp.choices[0].message.content)
print("Fingerprint:", resp.system_fingerprint)
OpenAI Node.js / TypeScript SDK
Hugging Face Transformers (local models)

When running an open-weights model locally with the Transformers library, determinism is controlled by seeding PyTorch and CUDA directly. The seed parameter concept translates directly into the numerical stack.

import torch
from transformers import pipeline

# Seed both CPU and GPU RNGs for maximum reproducibility
torch.manual_seed(42)
if torch.cuda.is_available():
    torch.cuda.manual_seed_all(42)

# Also disable CUDA non-deterministic algorithms
torch.backends.cudnn.deterministic = True
torch.backends.cudnn.benchmark = False

generator = pipeline("text-generation", model="meta-llama/Llama-3.2-1B",
                     device_map="auto")

# do_sample=False is the local equivalent of temperature=0
out = generator("Explain reinforcement learning in one sentence.",
                max_new_tokens=60, do_sample=False)
print(out[0]["generated_text"])
LangChain
LangChain's chat model wrappers accept a
model_kwargs
dict that passes through to the underlying API. Pass
seed
there alongside
temperature=0
for deterministic chains.

from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI

llm = ChatOpenAI(
    model="gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18",
    temperature=0,
    model_kwargs={"seed": 42},
)
response = llm.invoke("Describe the role of entropy in information theory.")
print(response.content)
Comparison with alternatives
Several techniques interact with or substitute for seed-based determinism depending on the goal:
Technique
How it works
Determinism level
Best for
seed + temperature=0
Fixes PRNG; greedy decoding
Best-effort (infra-scoped)
Eval suites, CI tests, debugging
temperature=0 only
Greedy decoding, no seed
Near-deterministic (GPU noise)
Simple reproducibility without seed support
Structured outputs / JSON mode
Constrains the output format
Format-deterministic; text still varies
Schema validation, structural testing
Prompt pinning
Lock prompt + examples exactly
No effect on sampling randomness
Preventing accidental prompt drift
Multi-sample + majority vote
Run N independent draws, pick consensus
Non-deterministic; reduces variance
Self-consistency, high-stakes decisions
Caching layer
Return stored response on cache hit
Fully deterministic (cache replay)
Cost reduction, speed, offline replay
Security & reliability

From a security perspective, seeds can introduce predictability risk in adversarial settings. If an attacker knows the seed used for an eval or content-generation pipeline, they can pre-compute what the model will output for a given prompt and craft prompts that produce specific behaviours. This is rarely a practical attack surface in typical LLM deployments, but it is worth keeping seeds internal to the system and not exposing them in API responses, logs accessible to end users, or client-side code.

Reliability considerations centre on the brittleness of exact-output tests. A test suite that asserts byte-identical outputs against a stored golden will break silently the moment the provider updates its infrastructure, producing false negatives (tests fail for infra reasons, not prompt regressions). The robust mitigation is schema-first evaluation: validate that outputs conform to an expected JSON schema, contain required entities, or satisfy classifiable quality criteria — any of which are robust to minor surface-form variation across model updates.
Keep seeds internal; do not expose them to end users or untrusted callers.
Treat a changed system_fingerprint as a reliability signal: re-validate stored golden outputs before trusting cached comparisons.
Never rely on seed-based determinism as a safety mechanism — it is not a security primitive.
Monitor system_fingerprint changes in production eval pipelines; set up alerts for unexpected shifts.
Document which eval results were produced under which fingerprint so historical comparisons remain valid.
Mental model

Think of an LLM's token-sampling step as rolling a weighted die at each position in the output sequence. The weights are determined by the model's learned parameters and the prompt; the actual roll is determined by the PRNG. Setting a seed is like telling the croupier to use a specific shuffled deck of dice outcomes rather than picking fresh ones. The same deck always produces the same roll sequence — but if the casino switches to a different brand of dice (model update), all bets are off.

Temperature=0 takes this analogy further: instead of rolling a die at all, the model simply picks the face with the highest printed number every time. No randomness remains; the seed becomes a safety net rather than the primary control. The combination — temperature=0 plus a seed — removes two distinct sources of non-determinism at once: stochastic sampling and PRNG initialisation variance. What remains is hardware-level floating-point variation, which only matters in practice when providers migrate serving infrastructure.
Key takeaways
A seed initialises the PRNG used during token sampling; combined with temperature=0 it provides the strongest determinism available through the API.
Seed-based determinism is best-effort: GPU floating-point non-associativity and provider infrastructure changes can produce different outputs even with an identical seed.
The system_fingerprint (OpenAI) encodes model version and backend config; always store it alongside generated outputs and treat a change as a signal to re-validate.
Pin the full dated model version string in eval and CI contexts; floating aliases resolve to different weights after provider updates.
Assert structural properties — JSON schema conformance, key presence, entity extraction — rather than exact byte-identical strings; the former survives minor model updates, the latter does not.
Cache responses keyed on (model, fingerprint, seed, prompt_hash) to cut eval costs by 70–90 % on runs where infrastructure is stable.
Seeds are for eval pipelines, CI tests, and debugging — not for production user-facing calls where output diversity is desirable.
For local open-weights models, seed the PyTorch and CUDA RNGs explicitly and set cudnn.deterministic=True; there is no API-level seed parameter.
Never rely on a seed as a security primitive; predictable outputs can be exploited if seeds are exposed to adversarial callers.
